Integrative Microbiology, Multi-Omics & Applied Bioinformatics

Making microbial systems visible, testable and traceable.

CLARITY LAB at UCD, led by Dr Guerrino Macori, integrates culture-dependent microbiology, direct-from-sample sequencing, multi-omics, emerging diagnostic tools and open bioinformatics to study microbial diversity, food safety and biological risk across the food chain, built environments, animals and human health contexts.

Explore our research Contact the lab

Explainer

What is microbial dark matter?

Many microorganisms remain difficult to grow, classify or interpret using standard methods. We study this hidden microbial diversity by combining culture-dependent microbiology, direct-from-sample sequencing, multi-omics and bioinformatics.

This allows us to investigate not only which microbes are present, but what they may be doing, how they interact and why they matter for food safety, antimicrobial resistance and One Health.

SampleCulture / sequenceInsight

Research

Research pillars

Our work connects microbial discovery, food-system safety, pathogen genomics, antimicrobial resistance and reproducible analysis to generate insight for public health and safer food systems.

01

Microbial Dark Matter & Model Microbiomes

We investigate hidden and poorly characterised microorganisms in complex microbial communities, using fermented foods and other model systems to understand microbial diversity, function and resilience.

02

Food Safety & Microbial Ecology

We study microbial communities across food systems to understand safety, quality, spoilage, persistence and risk.

03

Pathogen Genomics, AMR & One Health

We use microbiological, genomic and bioinformatic tools to track pathogens, antimicrobial resistance and food-chain risks across human, animal, environmental and built-environment contexts.

04

Methods, Multi-Omics & Open Bioinformatics

We develop and apply reproducible workflows to analyse complex microbial, genomic and multi-omics data, supporting microbial ecology, food safety and pathogen surveillance.

People & Culture

The lab team

CLARITY LAB at UCD is a collaborative research environment for students, researchers and partners working across microbiology, bioinformatics, food safety and public health.

Portrait placeholder
Dr Guerrino Macori

Principal Investigator

Dr Guerrino Macori

Assistant Professor, UCD College of Science / School of Biology and Environmental Science

Dr. Guerrino Macori is an Assistant Professor in Applied Bioinformatics at UCD and a Conway Fellow. His research group investigates the intersection of metagenomics, microbial ecology, and public health. The lab leverages next-generation sequencing and advanced computational workflows—including long-read technologies and metatranscriptomics—to address critical challenges across clinical, agricultural, and urban environments. Key research themes encompass the tracking of antimicrobial resistance, the promotion of sustainable urban food systems, and the genomic characterisation of pathogens within a One Health framework. Beyond technical innovation, Dr. Macori is a dedicated advocate for open scientific infrastructure, contributing to international consortiums like PHA4GE. His group is fundamentally committed to fostering an inclusive, progressive research culture, actively championing Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion in STEM and developing award-winning, open-source educational tools.

Mingwei Hong

PhD Candidate

Mingwei Hong

Computational Reconstruction of Synthetic Microbiomes for Predicting Pathogen Dynamics

Mingwei explores synthetic microbiomes as controlled model systems for investigating microbial community dynamics and pathogen behaviour.

Jacques Olivier

PhD Candidate

Jacques Olivier

AI-Enhanced Deciphering of Microbial Dark Matter

Jacques investigates microbial dark matter to understand how functional traits, particularly antimicrobial resistance, disseminate through complex microbiomes.

Contact and collaboration

We collaborate with academic, clinical, public-health, food-sector and computational partners on projects involving microbiology, sequencing, microbial ecology, genomic epidemiology, antimicrobial resistance and food-system safety.
